original_hypothesis.title: setLockDuration Bypass Allows Immediate Withdrawal of Time-Locked Tokens
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The setLockDuration function validates new unlock time using `block.timestamp + _duration >= old unlockTime` but computes the actual new unlockTime as `lastLockTime + _duration`. As time passes since lastLockTime, the gap between block.timestamp and lastLockTime widens, allowing users to pass the validation check with a small duration that sets unlockTime to a past timestamp, enabling immediate withdrawal.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This directly violates the author-stated invariant 'Users must not be able to reduce their previously set lockup times' and enables theft of lockdrop NFT rewards by unlocking before the committed duration expires.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation uses `uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_duration) < unlockTime` but assignment uses `lastLockTime + uint32(_duration)` — these diverge as time passes

preconditions: User has tokens locked with quantity > 0
Sufficient time has elapsed since lastLockTime (past midpoint of lock duration)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Check: block.timestamp + _duration >= unlockTime. Assignment: unlockTime = lastLockTime + _duration. When block.timestamp >> lastLockTime, small _duration satisfies check but produces past unlockTime.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User locks 1000 ETH for 365 days during lockdrop, receives NFT rewards. At day 300, calls setLockDuration(66 days): check 300+66=366>=365 passes, but new unlockTime=0+66=day 66 (234 days in the past). Calls unlock immediately.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker retains all lockdrop NFT rewards (worth the full locked amount / nftCost NFTs) while recovering 100% of locked principal after only a fraction of the committed duration.

economic_questions: What is the exact time threshold at which a user can begin reducing their unlock time?
What is the value of NFT rewards relative to the opportunity cost of the shortened lock?
Can this be combined with the phantom remainder carry-over to amplify NFT farming?
validation_requirements: Prove lock-then-early-unlock sequence with specific timestamps in a PoC
Calculate NFT reward value for typical lock amounts against nftCost configurations
Verify that any user past the midpoint of their lock can unlock immediately

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Any amount of lockable tokens (scales with desired NFT count)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All lockdrop NFT rewards for the locked amount, with near-zero effective lock commitment
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Effectively infinite ROI on lock commitment (NFT rewards for free)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Two transactions: setLockDuration + unlock (can be same block)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None — uses user's own locked tokens
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After passing the midpoint of any lock duration
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; purely on-chain state manipulation

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol and honest lockers who committed to full lock durations
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Lockdrop NFT dilution proportional to exploiter's share of total locks
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire TVL of locked tokens can be withdrawn early; all lockdrop NFT integrity compromised

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: lastLockTime vs block.timestamp divergence; MaxLockDuration from configStorage
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Prove that for lockDuration D locked at time T0, at time T0+D/2+1, the user can set duration to D/2 and unlock

validation_result.impact_summary: Any user who has waited past the midpoint of their lock duration can call setLockDuration to set their unlockTime to a past timestamp, enabling immediate withdrawal of all locked tokens while retaining any lockdrop NFT rewards.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The setLockDuration function contains a critical logic flaw where the validation check and the state assignment use different reference timestamps. The validation checks `uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_duration) < unlockTime` (i.e., passes when block.timestamp + duration >= old unlockTime), but the new unlockTime is computed as `lastLockTime + uint32(_duration)`. As time elapses since lastLockTime, the gap (block.timestamp - lastLockTime) grows, allowing ever-smaller durations to pass the check while producing an unlockTime that is in the past. Concretely: a user who locked at T0 for D seconds has unlockTime = T0 + D and lastLockTime = T0. At time T1 = T0 + elapsed, setting newDuration = D - elapsed + 1 passes the check (T1 + newDuration = T0 + D + 1 >= T0 + D), but the new unlockTime = T0 + D - elapsed + 1 = T1 - elapsed + (D - elapsed + 1), which equals T1 - (2*elapsed - D - 1). Once elapsed > D/2, the user can find a duration that passes the check but produces a past unlockTime. The unlock function simply checks `lockedToken.unlockTime > uint32(block.timestamp)`, so any past unlockTime allows immediate withdrawal.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 245-270 of LockManager.sol clearly show the discrepancy. The check on line 256-259 uses block.timestamp while the assignment on lines 264-266 uses lastLockTime. These are not equivalent when time has passed since the lock was created. The unlock function at lines 316-320 confirms that a past unlockTime allows withdrawal.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The validation check uses block.timestamp + _duration >= old unlockTime, but the assignment uses lastLockTime + _duration. Since lastLockTime < block.timestamp (time has passed since locking), a carefully chosen _duration can pass the check while producing a new unlockTime that is in the past relative to block.timestamp.
The unlock function only checks that unlockTime is not in the future. If setLockDuration has set unlockTime to a past timestamp, this check passes and the user can withdraw immediately.

calling_sequence: 1. User locks tokens via lock() at time T0 with duration D. lockedToken.lastLockTime = T0, lockedToken.unlockTime = T0 + D (LockManager.sol:365-368)
2. At time T1 = T0 + elapsed (where elapsed > D/2), user calls setLockDuration(newDuration) where newDuration = D - elapsed + delta (small delta)
3. Check at line 256-259: uint32(T1) + uint32(newDuration) = T1 + D - elapsed + delta >= T0 + D when delta >= T0 + D - T1 - D + elapsed = elapsed - (T1 - T0) = 0. Check passes.
4. Assignment at lines 264-266: unlockTime = T0 + newDuration = T0 + D - elapsed + delta = (T0 + D) - elapsed + delta
5. New unlockTime = T1 - 2*elapsed + D + delta. When elapsed > D/2, this is less than T1 (current time). unlockTime is now in the past.
6. User calls unlock() at line 311. Check at line 319: unlockTime <= uint32(block.timestamp) passes. Tokens are transferred.
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The core issue is the divergence between the check basis (block.timestamp) and the assignment basis (lastLockTime) in setLockDuration. The check at line 256-259 ensures block.timestamp + _duration >= old unlockTime, which is satisfied with _duration >= (old unlockTime - block.timestamp). But the assignment at lines 264-266 computes lastLockTime + _duration. Since lastLockTime is in the past relative to block.timestamp, the resulting unlockTime = lastLockTime + _duration can be much less than block.timestamp. For example: lock at timestamp 1000 with 365-day duration (31536000 seconds). unlockTime = 1000 + 31536000 = 31537000. At timestamp 31236000 (300 days elapsed, 65 days remaining): setLockDuration(31537000 - 31236000) = setLockDuration(301000) passes check (31236000 + 301000 = 31537000 >= 31537000). New unlockTime = 1000 + 301000 = 302000, which is ~358 days in the past. User unlocks immediately.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that contradict or are relevant to this vulnerability. The start-lockdrop.ts script shows lockdrops are configured with 30-day windows, and configure-token-test.ts shows tokens are configured with specific nftCost values, confirming the lockdrop and token infrastructure exists as described.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Concrete scenario validated: User locks 1000 ETH during lockdrop with 365-day duration at timestamp T0. lastLockTime = T0, unlockTime = T0 + 365d. User receives NFTs based on locked amount and nftCost. At T0 + 300d, user calls setLockDuration(66d). Check: (T0+300d) + 66d = T0+366d >= T0+365d → passes. Assignment: unlockTime = T0 + 66d, which is 234 days in the past. User calls unlock() in same or next transaction. unlockTime (T0+66d) <= block.timestamp (T0+300d) → passes. User withdraws all 1000 ETH, keeping lockdrop NFT rewards. Attack cost: ~2 transactions of gas. Any user past the midpoint of their lock can exploit this.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. Every locked position in the contract is vulnerable once sufficient time has elapsed (past the midpoint of the lock duration). Users can: (1) withdraw locked tokens far before their committed unlock time, (2) retain any lockdrop NFT rewards that were granted based on the original lock commitment, (3) immediately re-lock with a new long duration to farm more NFT rewards and repeat. This completely undermines the time-lock mechanism and lockdrop reward integrity. Total TVL of locked tokens is at risk of premature withdrawal.
recommended_mitigations: Fix the validation check to compare the actual new unlockTime against the old unlockTime: replace `uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_duration) < lockedTokens[msg.sender][tokenContract].unlockTime` with `lastLockTime + uint32(_duration) < lockedTokens[msg.sender][tokenContract].unlockTime`
Additionally ensure the new unlockTime is not in the past: add check `require(lastLockTime + uint32(_duration) >= uint32(block.timestamp), 'unlock time in past')`

Consider using block.timestamp as the base for unlockTime calculation instead of lastLockTime: `lockedTokens[msg.sender][tokenContract].unlockTime = uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_duration)` to match the validation semantics
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockManager with a configStorage mock providing MaxLockDuration = 365 days. 2. Configure a token and set up accounts. 3. Lock tokens at T0 with 365-day duration. 4. Advance block.timestamp to T0 + 300 days. 5. Call setLockDuration(66 days). Verify it succeeds. 6. Check lockedTokens unlockTime = T0 + 66 days (in the past). 7. Call unlock() and verify tokens are returned immediately despite 65 days remaining on original lock.
uncertainties: MaxLockDuration config value is not shown in code but must be >= the duration used in the attack for it to pass the first check
The exact lockdrop NFT reward value depends on nftCost configuration and downstream NFT valuation
additional_testing_needed: Verify exact boundary conditions for when the exploit becomes possible (elapsed > D/2 threshold)
Test with multiple tokens locked simultaneously to ensure all unlockTimes are affected
Verify interaction with _lock function - can a user re-lock after exploiting setLockDuration to farm additional NFT rewards in a subsequent lockdrop
